chunk_summary: This section addresses pediatric nutrition strategies for chronic kidney and liver disease in India, emphasizing practical adaptations to resource constraints, malnutrition, and food insecurity while prioritizing growth and metabolic stability.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of India-specific health-system constraints on pediatric nutrition prescriptions for chronic kidney and liver disease.
Learn strategies to manage sodium balance in CAKUT-pattern CKD and nephrotic disease in Indian children.
Develop resource-sensitive nutrition plans, including protein quality optimization and phosphate management in lacto-vegetarian diets.
Recognize the importance of explicit tube-feeding thresholds and monitoring datasets in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Children with chronic organ disease in India often face dual burdens: disease-related malnutrition and background undernutrition.
Sodium wasting in CAKUT-pattern CKD may require supplementation despite normal serum sodium levels.
Protein goals in lacto-vegetarian diets can be met with milk, curd, paneer, eggs (if acceptable), and soy, with careful phosphate management.
Resource constraints may limit access to renal/hepatic formulas and modular supplements; substitutions and densification recipes are critical.
Delayed dialysis and long transplant waits necessitate nutrition strategies to prevent irreversible stunting and sarcopenia.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: In India, children with chronic organ disease often enter care with a dual burden: disease-related malnutrition plus background undernutrition, food insecurity, and intermittent access to dietetic support. (Source: Chronic kidney disease in children: An Indian perspective — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4921439/)
document_key_topics: Pediatric nutrition in chronic kidney and liver disease
